# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Regional Mayoral Candidate

## 0. Assumptions

This brief is Mode A (full-brief mode) — all 8 fields were supplied. No defaults applied. The table below records explicitly stated values for auditability.

| Field | Value from brief |
|---|---|
| Sector | Political/civic — local government, regional mayoral race |
| Outcome | Win election day majority against 3-term incumbent backed by ruling national party |
| Audience | Registered voters in city of 180,000: young professionals 25–40 (returning from capital/abroad), small business owners, public-sector workers, retirees |
| Competition | 3-term incumbent with ruling national party backing; €1.2M+ direct campaign budget plus estimated €3–5M equivalent in state-media coverage |
| Budget | €25,000 total over 6 months |
| Existing channels | 12k Facebook followers (2-year council record), 800-member Discord (young professionals), relationships with 5 independent local Facebook groups (~45k combined reach), 60 completed in-person neighborhood meetings |
| Bottleneck | Visibility beyond existing base; trust strong inside the base, unknown outside it; local media partially captured |
| Time window | 6 months to election day |
| Capacity | Candidate + campaign manager + 2 paid staff (40+ hrs/week each); ~30 active volunteers (~4 hrs/week each = ~120 volunteer-hours/week). Total team capacity: ~280 hrs/week |

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas

Five concepts across different archetypes. The brief does not name the incumbent by name — all competitor references use role-based descriptors.

---

### Concept A — "The Neighbor, Not the Notice"
**Archetype:** Founder-Story Arc + Door-to-Door / Localized

**One-line thesis:** The 60 neighborhood meetings the candidate already ran are the campaign — double down on face-to-face, candidate-led presence in every district as the anti-billboard, anti-broadcast alternative that the incumbent's machine cannot credibly replicate.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (volunteer networks, candidate personal social presence, in-person district visits) with Tier 2 (organic TikTok + Facebook video from each visit).

**Authenticity hook:** Candidate's existing track record as a city council member — specific decisions, specific votes, specific neighborhood problems they showed up for. The incumbent has state-media reach; the candidate has two years of showing up on foot.

**Minimum viable version:** Pick the three neighborhoods with the most unresolved, visible issues (broken infrastructure, underfunded services). Candidate spends one day in each this week, films a 90-second walk-and-talk from the street. No studio. No script. Posts it personally. Measures organic reach and volunteer sign-ups from that single day.

---

### Concept B — "What €1.2M Buys"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative

**One-line thesis:** The incumbent's spending asymmetry is the vulnerability — name it openly, document it numerically, and position the candidate's absence from billboards and state media as the honest signal.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (candidate voice, volunteer-distributed print) + Tier 2 (organic Facebook Groups, earned media with independent local and regional press).

**Authenticity hook:** A specific, documented running tally — "The incumbent's campaign has now spent an estimated [X] on billboard space alone this month. Here is what that money could have funded instead." The concrete number grounds the counter-narrative in verifiable fact.

**Minimum viable version:** A single Facebook post from the candidate with one specific, sourced expenditure figure and one specific public service it could have funded. Track shares and group-crosspost reach over 48 hours before deciding whether to build a recurring counter-spend thread.

---

### Concept C — "The Return Compact"
**Archetype:** Community-Build + Coalition Play

**One-line thesis:** The young professionals who left the city and came back (or stayed) are the candidate's most credible advocates — build a named coalition around them, give them a specific platform commitment, and let peer-to-peer trust carry it into wider social circles.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (the existing 800-member Discord as the spine, expanded; volunteer ambassador network from this cohort) + Tier 2 (TikTok and Instagram content by and for this audience).

**Authenticity hook:** Real returnees with specific stories of what brought them back, what they're missing, what they'd stay for. Not demographic talking points — named people (with consent) telling one concrete thing they want from local government.

**Minimum viable version:** A 7-day "return stories" thread in the Discord asking members to share the one thing they came back for and the one thing local government should fix. The best 3–5 responses become the first content posts and the seed of a policy commitment document.

---

### Concept D — "The Five Voices"
**Archetype:** Earned-Media Stunt + Coalition Play

**One-line thesis:** Recruit five credible, locally known individuals — one from each key voter segment — who publicly endorse the candidate and tell their own Self/Us/Now story, turning the endorsement into a week-long earned-media moment rather than a logo list.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (earned media in independent local press, organic Facebook Group distribution) + Tier 1 (candidate sharing and responding to each voice personally).

**Authenticity hook:** The five voices cannot be campaign staff or obvious political allies — one small business owner, one public-sector worker, one retiree, one young professional, one community leader from a neighborhood that has historically voted for the incumbent. The diversity of the panel is the story.

**Minimum viable version:** Secure one endorsement first — the most credible and least expected person available. Produce one 2-minute interview video (phone footage, no editing beyond trimming). Release it and measure the earned-media pickup from the 5 independent local Facebook groups before scaling.

---

### Concept E — "The Shadow Budget"
**Archetype:** Fundraising Drive + Counter-Narrative

**One-line thesis:** Run a public citizen fundraising campaign for the candidate — not primarily to raise money (€25k is already in place), but to generate a documented count of individual donors as a proof of democratic mandate that no state-media budget can fake.

**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (direct ask from candidate to warm Facebook and Discord audiences, peer-to-peer sharing by volunteers) + Tier 2 (earned media on the donor-count milestone, not the money amount).

**Authenticity hook:** Report donor count publicly and frequently — "3,200 individual citizens have contributed to this campaign; the incumbent's campaign is funded by [ruling party] and state-adjacent sources." The number is the story, not the revenue.

**Minimum viable version:** A 72-hour "founding supporter" drive targeting the existing 12k Facebook followers and 800 Discord members with a €5 minimum. Track donor count, not total raised. Success signal: 200+ donors in 72 hours confirms the warm-base activation rate.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

**This deliverable assumes the campaign selects a hybrid of Concepts A and B as the primary strategy**, with Concept C's community-build layer as the organizational infrastructure and Concept D's earned-media endorsement model as a recurring mid-campaign activation.

Rationale: at categorical asymmetry, the counter-narrative (B) and door-to-door/localized (A) archetypes are the two highest-leverage tools the political/civic sector rider explicitly names. The community node in Concept C is already partially built (Discord + Facebook groups) and should be the organizing spine, not a separate campaign. Concept D provides earned-media moments every 4–6 weeks without requiring budget.

Concept E (fundraising) is recommended as a secondary activation in Month 2 specifically to generate the donor-count statistic — not as a primary campaign shape.

If the campaign selects a different concept, the Stage 4–5 channel stack and action list should be adjusted accordingly. The asymmetry verdict and MMF gate below apply regardless of concept selection.

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

**Your asymmetry is categorical (competitor spend: candidate spend ≈ 200:1 in direct campaign budget, and non-comparable when state-media equivalent reach of €3–5M is included).** This means broad paid channels are off the table — the game is volunteer networks, candidate-led organic presence, earned media in the independent press, and counter-positioning that turns the incumbent's saturation into a liability.

The asymmetry is non-comparable at the state-media level. Even stripping out state-media coverage and looking only at the direct campaign budgets, the ratio (€1.2M vs. €25k) is 48:1, which is categorical. Adding €3–5M in equivalent state-media reach makes the true ratio effectively uncountable by conventional advertising metrics. This is the same structural condition as the Tisza/Fidesz dynamic, appropriately scaled to a regional race.

---

## 3a. Message-Market-Fit Gate

Scoring against the three MMF questions based on information in the brief:

**Question 1 — Revenue/commitment signal:** Have ≥10 people in the target audience committed (volunteered, signed up, directly asked) for what is being offered?

**Yes.** The brief reports 30 active volunteers and 60 completed in-person neighborhood meetings. Commitment is demonstrated. Score: 1/1.

**Question 2 — Language signal:** Can you name a specific pain the audience articulates in their own words before you pitch them?

**Inferred yes, not confirmed in the brief.** The brief identifies key audience segments and their likely concerns but does not supply a quoted pain sentence from a member of the target audience. The 60 neighborhood meetings almost certainly produced this material — but it is not named in the brief. Score: 0.5/1 (treat as borderline pending confirmation).

**Question 3 — Close signal:** If you asked 5 people in the target audience to commit today, would at least 3 say yes?

**Yes.** Thirty active volunteers already committed. The warm base in the Discord and Facebook groups has been built over two years. Score: 1/1.

**MMF Verdict: Borderline (2.5/3 — treat as 2/3).**

The weak link is Question 2: the campaign needs to surface and name the specific language the target segments use to describe their grievances — not the campaign's framing of those grievances, but the audience's own words. This is almost certainly already present in notes from the 60 neighborhood meetings. The Week 1 action below (see Section 8) includes extracting this language before any content is published.

This does not block the campaign plan. The campaign proceeds, but the first-30-days action list opens with a language-capture step as a pre-distribution gate.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function: Trust compounding.** The bottleneck is not demand — the election creates its own attention window — and it is not reach in aggregate, it is credibility and reach *beyond the existing base* into undecided and soft-incumbent voters. Trust compounding is the right function. The political/civic sector rider (Rider 5) confirms this and prioritizes ground game, Tier 1 community nodes, and counter-media in information-dark zones.

**Sector rider applied:** Political/Civic (Rider 5). Key adjustments:
- Archetype defaults: founder-story arc (candidate's lived experience via Ganz Self/Us/Now), counter-narrative, coalition play, earned-media stunt.
- Channel weighting: ground game is the highest-leverage channel. Volunteer door-knock is ~45× more persuasive than a paid impression. Every €1 spent on volunteer coordination and training produces more persuasion than any amount of Meta spend.
- Failure-mode warning: fragmented opposition. The brief describes a single consolidated candidate — this precondition is met. Hold the consolidated frame. Do not let coalition-building dilute the candidate's message identity.

**Channel stack (prioritized, categorical asymmetry — Tier 1 and 2 only):**

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Weekly effort | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volunteer ground network (door-knock, direct voter contact) | 1 | 120 hrs/week (30 volunteers × 4 hrs) | Trust compounding — peer persuasion, the highest-leverage channel in elections |
| 2 | Candidate personal Facebook presence (Lives, posts from field locations) | 1 | 5 hrs/week candidate time | Trust compounding — parasocial credibility, unpolished field footage |
| 3 | 5 independent local Facebook Groups | 2 | 3 hrs/week (campaign manager) | Reach expansion — the last organic surface on Meta with meaningful reach |
| 4 | Discord community (800-member young professionals) | 1 | 2 hrs/week candidate, 2 hrs/week campaign manager | Trust compounding + mobilization of the highest-activation audience segment |
| 5 | Organic TikTok (candidate + volunteers) | 2 | 3 hrs/week (campaign manager + volunteers) | Reach expansion — young professional segment, algorithmic amplification potential |
| 6 | Earned media (independent local and regional press) | 2 | 4 hrs/week (campaign manager) | Borrowed credibility — the independent press is the only non-captured media channel |
| 7 | Volunteer-distributed print (neighborhood-specific one-pagers, counter-media in low-internet areas) | 1 | 10 hrs/week (volunteers) | Trust compounding in media-dark segments — retirees, low-digital neighborhoods |
| 8 | Organic Instagram (Stories + Reels from field) | 2 | 2 hrs/week | Reach with young professionals returning from abroad |

**Total core team effort:** ~31 hrs/week above volunteer hours. Fits within the 40+ hrs/week of candidate + campaign manager + 2 paid staff.

**Paid channels:** None. Categorical asymmetry. Broad cold paid is refused (see Section 9).

---

## 5. Allocation

**95% organic / 5% operational (no paid media)**

With €25,000 over 6 months and categorical asymmetry, the allocation is not a paid vs. organic split — it is a question of where the €25,000 goes to maximize organic effectiveness:

| Category | Estimated allocation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Volunteer infrastructure (training, coordination tools, transport to remote neighborhoods) | €6,000 (24%) | Every €1 here multiplies into hundreds of persuasive door-knock conversations |
| Print materials (voter-specific leaflets, "Tiszta Hang"-style counter-media for media-dark areas) | €5,000 (20%) | Reaches retirees and low-digital segments the incumbent controls via state TV |
| Events and rally production (audio, basic signage — no agency, no studio) | €4,000 (16%) | Generates earned-media moments and organic volunteer-content footage |
| Campaign tools (CRM for voter contact tracking, basic website, UTM-tagging analytics) | €3,000 (12%) | Infrastructure for Template 5 measurement and volunteer coordination |
| Earned-media production (photography, video gear for candidate field content — not agency) | €2,500 (10%) | Enables candidate-led unpolished content at higher reliability |
| Contingency + final-72-hours GOTV operations | €4,500 (18%) | Turnout in the last 72 hours is the ground game's final leverage point |

**No paid advertising budget.** This is not a budget constraint — it is the correct strategic choice. At 200:1 asymmetry, any paid digital spend would be invisible against the incumbent's saturation and would divert resources from the ground network, which is the only channel that can match the incumbent's paid reach with higher persuasion depth.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

**Note:** The brief does not name the incumbent. All references below use role-based descriptors.

### What [the incumbent] saturates

The incumbent saturates: state television and local state-adjacent programming (daily presence, favorable framing, no unedited access for the challenger); regional billboard placements (stability messaging, photographs of delivered infrastructure); paid Facebook advertising in local feeds (carousel and video ads, production-polished, targeted to older demographic segments); sponsored supplements in local newspapers (mixed with editorial content, visually indistinguishable from journalism); and social media presence with professional agency management (consistent posting, curated visual identity, high production value).

Emotional register of incumbent's saturation: **continuity, security, done deals.** "We built X. We are building Y. Change is a risk."

Visual style: professional photography, official settings, delivered infrastructure as backdrop, candidate in formal or semi-formal attire. High production value throughout.

Message tropes: stability, experience, national party backing as a resource not a liability, delivered projects as proof of competence.

### The absence that becomes your signal

The incumbent's saturation of polished, paid, state-adjacent media channels means **their absence from authentic, unmediated, real-time formats is the gap**. The incumbent is not filming from neighborhoods at 7am. The incumbent is not answering unmoderated questions in a Discord with 800 young professionals. The incumbent is not posting unedited footage of a conversation with a small business owner in the market.

The candidate's deliberate, visible absence from billboards, state media, and high-production content is the positioning — but only if it is named and owned. An absence that is not explained reads as limited resources. An absence that is explicitly framed reads as a choice.

**The positioning frame:** "We don't have €1.2M for billboards. We have [X] neighbors willing to knock on doors." The specific number turns a weakness into a credential.

The counter-position is also specifically: every time the incumbent runs a billboard, runs a polished ad, runs a sponsored supplement, the candidate's response is to be visibly, demonstrably doing the opposite — in the street, in the neighborhood, on the phone, in the Discord, in the 90-second phone-filmed video.

### One-sentence positioning line

**"Every euro this campaign has goes to neighbors talking to neighbors — not to billboards, not to consultants, not to sponsored supplements."**

This line must hold across every content piece. It is the test for any proposed campaign action: does this action reinforce or contradict the line?

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

Each shape includes a flagship Self/Us/Now piece of content.

---

### Shape 1 — Community-First (Start with the 800, grow through word-of-mouth)

**Core logic:** The Discord is the highest-activation, most underutilized channel in the existing stack. Young professionals who left and returned (or stayed) are both a voter segment and a volunteer multiplier — they have social capital in multiple city networks simultaneously. Turn the Discord from an information channel into a mobilization spine.

**Flagship Self/Us/Now content:**
- *Self:* "I took a city-council salary that is a third of what I was earning before. Here is why I did it, and what I saw from the inside that made me stay." — candidate, filmed at their actual desk in city council, not a studio.
- *Us:* "You came back, or you stayed, or you're thinking about leaving. Every one of us who made that choice did it because we thought this city was worth it. The question is whether local government thinks the same."
- *Now:* "We're hosting a two-hour planning session in the Discord this Thursday. Bring the one thing you think local government keeps getting wrong. We're putting it in the platform document."

**How it works:**
- Months 1–2: Discord becomes a two-way policy conversation, not a broadcast channel. Candidate posts daily for 30 days (no exceptions). Extracts specific policy language from member contributions.
- Months 2–4: Discord members become volunteer ambassadors, each responsible for 2–3 personal conversations per week with their own networks (peer-to-peer extends the 12k Facebook reach without algorithm dependence).
- Months 4–6: Discord graduates to a GOTV coordination tool. Member-organized watch parties, polling-station transportation, election-day check-ins.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Strongest with the 25–40 segment; requires supplementary strategy for retirees and low-digital neighborhoods.
- Slower to build than earned-media stunts — requires 30-day founder presence that cannot be delegated.
- Risk: if the candidate misses daily posting for more than 3 days in the seed phase, the community loses momentum and is hard to restart.

---

### Shape 2 — Earned-Media-First (One newsworthy action drives press + organic amplification)

**Core logic:** The independent local press is the one non-captured media channel. A single newsworthy action — a specific accountability moment tied to a documented fact — generates earned coverage that reaches the audiences the candidate cannot reach through owned channels (particularly older voters who rely on print and TV).

**Flagship Self/Us/Now content:**
- *Self:* "I sat in city council and watched the vote on [specific infrastructure decision that affected residents]. I voted no. Here is what I saw and what happened after." — candidate, walking through the location in question, phone-filmed.
- *Us:* "Every person who has a [specific service problem — road, clinic, school, parking] in this city has asked why nothing has changed for three terms. I looked at the budget. Here is the answer."
- *Now:* "I am publishing the full financial comparison at [specific URL] today. Share it before it gets buried."

**How it works:**
- Month 1: Identify the single most documented, verifiable, locally felt policy failure from the incumbent's three terms. This requires research using publicly available council records and budget documents.
- Month 2: Organize a press moment — candidate at the location of the failure, with documentation, available for unscripted questions. Pitch independently to the independent local press, regional press, and any national reporters covering local elections.
- Month 3 onward: Repeat with one accountability moment per month. Each moment becomes a Facebook Group thread, a Discord debrief, and a volunteer talking-point sheet.

**Tradeoffs:**
- High ceiling: one strong earned-media moment can reach the retiree and low-digital segments that organic TikTok and Discord cannot.
- High risk: if the documented failure is contested, the campaign must be able to defend every number publicly under hostile questioning. Research quality is the constraint.
- Requires identifying an independent press contact (or national reporter) who will cover an accountability story in a local race — that relationship needs to be built in Month 1.

---

### Shape 3 — Ground-Game-First (Maximize the volunteer-to-persuasion multiplier before any digital strategy)

**Core logic:** At categorical asymmetry, the political/civic sector rider is explicit: a volunteer door-knock is ~45× more persuasive than a paid impression. Thirty volunteers at 4 hours per week, well-trained, deployed in target precincts, will produce more vote swing than any combination of digital organic content at the candidate's follower scale.

**Flagship Self/Us/Now content:**
- *Self:* "I have spent two years asking questions at city council and getting non-answers. Last month I went door-to-door in [specific neighborhood] and spent an afternoon listening instead. What I heard in three hours was more useful than two years of meetings."
- *Us:* "The people who live in [neighborhood] don't need to be told their water pressure is bad or that the bus stop is broken. They need someone who comes to them, not to a press conference."
- *Now:* "We need 30 more volunteers by the end of this month to cover every district in the city. The link is below. It takes 4 hours a week and a willingness to knock on a neighbor's door."

**How it works:**
- Month 1: Recruit and train the volunteer corps. Target: 60 total (double current 30). Train on: the candidate's story, the three key policy contrasts vs. the incumbent, how to handle hostile doors, how to log contacts.
- Months 2–5: Systematic precinct coverage. Prioritize precincts where the incumbent's margin in the last election was smallest (swing precincts). Log every contact in a simple CRM.
- Month 6: GOTV sprint. Every volunteer contacts every registered supporter 3× in the final 10 days — letter, door-knock, phone call. Election-day polling-station operations.
- Digital runs in parallel but in support of ground game: candidate social content gives volunteers something to share; Discord gives volunteers a coordination channel; TikTok gives the campaign credibility with the segment that is never home when volunteers knock.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Highest ceiling for total vote swing — this is the mechanism that won Hungary, scaled appropriately.
- Slowest to build: volunteer recruitment and training in Month 1 is the critical path. If the 60-volunteer target is not met by Month 2, the precinct coverage model fails.
- Requires a volunteer coordination infrastructure (CRM, training cadence, regular check-in meetings) that costs time and some operational budget.

**This is the recommended primary shape.** Shapes 1 (community-first) and 2 (earned-media-first) should run in parallel as supporting layers, not competing strategies.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Based on Shape 3 (Ground-Game-First) as primary, with Shapes 1 and 2 running in parallel.**

**Capacity ceiling:** Core team 40+ hrs/week × 4 people = 160 hrs/week available. Volunteers: 30 × 4 hrs = 120 hrs/week. Total: ~280 hrs/week. The plan below uses approximately 180–200 hrs/week, leaving buffer for unplanned events, press responses, and candidate scheduling conflicts.

---

### Week 1 — Infrastructure and Language Capture

**Goal:** Extract the audience's language before producing any content. Build coordination infrastructure before deploying volunteers.

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Review notes from 60 neighborhood meetings. Extract the exact sentences residents used to describe their problems — not the campaign's summary, the residents' words. Produce a ranked list of the top 10 phrases. | Campaign manager | 6 hrs | A document with ≥10 verbatim resident quotes, tagged by neighborhood and voter segment |
| Set up voter contact CRM (simple spreadsheet or NationBuilder equivalent) with precinct-level data. Identify the 5 swing precincts where the margin was smallest last election. | Paid staff #1 | 8 hrs | Functional CRM with precinct list, volunteer assignment structure |
| UTM-tag all existing links (Facebook, Discord, any external website). Set up basic analytics source tracking so every conversion can be attributed to a channel. | Paid staff #2 | 4 hrs | Every campaign URL includes UTM parameters; analytics dashboard shows per-channel conversions |
| Candidate posts the first Self/Us/Now video — filmed at a location relevant to the top resident grievance identified in the meeting notes. No script. Maximum 3 minutes. Posted to personal Facebook, Discord, and shared into the 5 Facebook Groups. | Candidate | 3 hrs filming + posting | ≥50 organic shares within 48 hours; ≥5 comments that contain resident language (not praise) |
| Post in Discord: "We are building the platform this week. What is the one thing you wish local government would fix? Be specific." Pin the thread. Candidate replies personally to every response for 7 days. | Candidate | 2 hrs/day × 7 = 14 hrs | ≥80 replies from Discord members; ≥20 specific policy requests that can be used in platform language |
| Contact the top 3 independent local press contacts with a one-sentence pitch: "The candidate who ran 60 neighborhood meetings is available for an unscripted conversation about what residents told them. No press release. No spin." Outreach via email, Tuesday morning. | Campaign manager | 2 hrs | ≥1 reply expressing interest; success is a scheduled interview, not a published piece yet |
| Volunteer recruitment: Post in Discord and Facebook Groups asking for 30 additional volunteers. Post includes the specific ask (4 hrs/week, door-to-door, specific training date). | Campaign manager | 2 hrs | ≥15 expressions of interest by end of Week 1 |

**Week 1 effort total:** ~45 hrs core team + 30 volunteer hrs (existing volunteers on standby this week). Under capacity ceiling.

---

### Week 2 — Volunteer Training and First Ground Deployment

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Run volunteer training session (2 hours, in-person). Cover: candidate story in Self/Us/Now format, top 3 policy contrasts vs. incumbent, how to handle hostile doors, how to log contacts in CRM. Provide a one-page talking-point sheet using the resident language extracted in Week 1. | Campaign manager + candidate (30 min appearance) | 4 hrs preparation + 2 hrs session | ≥25 volunteers trained; ≥20 CRM log entries after first deployment |
| First precinct deployment: assign trained volunteers to the top 2 swing precincts. Goal: 50 voter contacts per precinct this week. | Paid staff #1 (coordination) | 4 hrs coordination | ≥100 voter contacts logged with notes |
| Candidate does a 30-minute Facebook Live from one of the swing precinct streets (unscripted, phone footage, responds to comments live). | Candidate | 30 min + 30 min prep | ≥500 live viewers or replay views; ≥30 comments; campaign manager clips 3 best moments for Discord and TikTok |
| First earned-media moment: if press contact from Week 1 replied, schedule and conduct the interview. Focus on the resident-language quotes — not campaign talking points. | Candidate + campaign manager | 3 hrs | Published piece or confirmed publication date |
| Produce and distribute a neighborhood-specific one-pager for the two swing precincts. Uses resident language from Week 1. No stock images. Photos from the candidate's actual neighborhood visits. | Paid staff #2 | 6 hrs | Printed and in volunteer hands before door-knocking starts |
| Discord: Candidate summarizes Week 1 policy responses and names the top 5 that will go into the platform. Tags the members who suggested them. | Candidate | 1 hr | ≥30 reactions; ≥10 members sharing the platform document externally |

**Week 2 effort total:** ~50 hrs core team + 100 volunteer-hours. Under capacity ceiling.

---

### Week 3 — Precinct Expansion and Content Systematization

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Expand to all 5 swing precincts. Each volunteer has a daily contact quota (10 doors per 4-hour shift). | Paid staff #1 | 4 hrs coordination | ≥250 voter contacts logged across all 5 precincts |
| Candidate does 2 unscripted TikTok videos this week: one "walk and talk" from a neighborhood, one response to the most common resident concern from the meeting notes. 90 seconds max. Filmed on phone, posted without agency edits. | Candidate + campaign manager | 2 hrs | ≥1,000 organic views per video within 72 hours; ≥20 saves or shares (saves matter more than likes) |
| Begin "Five Voices" sequence (from Concept D): identify the first endorser — the least expected, most credible person available from the target voter segments. Have a 1-hour conversation with them. Do not ask for a formal endorsement yet — just listen. | Candidate | 2 hrs | A named person who has agreed to continue the conversation |
| Campaign manager runs weekly 30-minute check-in with volunteers: what did you hear at doors this week? What objections came up most? Update the talking-point sheet with new language. | Campaign manager | 2 hrs | Updated talking-point sheet distributed before next deployment |
| Earned media: pitch a second independent press contact (regional, not just local). Pitch hook tied to the precinct deployment story — "30 neighbors knocking on doors vs. a billboard campaign." Specific and surprising. Outreach Thursday via email. | Campaign manager | 2 hrs | ≥1 reply; success signal is a scheduled call |
| Facebook Groups: campaign manager posts a weekly "what we heard this week" thread in each of the 5 groups. Uses resident language. No campaign jargon. Tags candidate. | Campaign manager | 3 hrs | ≥20 comments per post; ≥5 shares out of each group |

**Week 3 effort total:** ~50 hrs core team + 120 volunteer-hours. Under capacity ceiling.

---

### Week 4 — Consolidation, Platform Release, and First Earned-Media Moment

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Release the platform document — not as a press release, as a Discord announcement from the candidate in the first-person, citing the members who contributed. Simultaneously share in all 5 Facebook Groups. | Candidate | 2 hrs | ≥50 shares of the document across all channels; ≥100 Discord members engaging |
| First "Five Voices" video: film and release the endorsement from Week 3. 2-minute phone footage, unedited beyond trimming. Candidate shares personally with a 2-sentence note. | Campaign manager (filming) + candidate (post) | 3 hrs | ≥100 shares; ≥1 independent press pickup; ≥5 new volunteer sign-ups attributed to this content (ask "how did you hear about us?" in volunteer sign-up form) |
| Candidate attends and speaks at one public event not organized by the campaign — a small business owner meeting, a retiree center event, a neighborhood association meeting. Asks for 5 minutes, not a platform. | Candidate | 3 hrs | ≥10 new voter contacts from the event; at least one invitation to return |
| Volunteer debrief and recruitment: run a second training for the new cohort recruited in Weeks 1–2. Total volunteer target: 60. | Campaign manager | 4 hrs | ≥55 total active volunteers by end of Week 4 |
| Review Template 5 data: which channels are converting (sign-ups, volunteer applications, Discord joins)? Cut the bottom performer if it is below 20% of the top channel's conversion rate. | Paid staff #2 | 2 hrs | Per-channel conversion report; documented decision to reallocate effort |

**Week 4 effort total:** ~45 hrs core team + 120 volunteer-hours. Under capacity ceiling.

---

**Capacity check:** The draft plan runs 190–200 core-team hours/week across 4 people. Each person is averaging 47–50 hrs/week, within the "40+ hrs/week" stated capacity. If any team member's effective capacity drops, the following actions get cut first in order of priority: (1) TikTok content production (high-effort, medium-conversion so far), (2) second earned-media pitch in Week 3, (3) Facebook Live in Week 2. These are the re-add actions if capacity opens up later.

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**Paid advertising is refused at categorical asymmetry.**

This is not a budget recommendation — it is a strategic one. At a 200:1 direct budget ratio (and effectively non-comparable with state-media included), allocating any portion of the €25,000 to broad paid digital would:

1. Produce impressions at a rate several orders of magnitude below the incumbent's saturation, with zero detectable incremental persuasion effect.
2. Divert resources from the ground game, which is the only channel that can match the incumbent's paid reach per-unit persuasion depth.
3. Contradict the counter-positioning line: "Every euro goes to neighbors talking to neighbors." Any paid ad spend, even small, is directly inconsistent with the campaign's positioning.

The political/civic rider explicitly notes: turnout in the last 72 hours is a function of organized human contact, not ad spend. The €25,000 has higher expected value deployed in volunteer coordination, precinct operations, and GOTV logistics than in any paid channel.

**If the campaign encounters a specific, narrow scenario where paid might apply** — for example, if the Meta political ad ban does not extend to local government races in this jurisdiction, and if organic content clears the 24–48h traction gate with strong saves and shares — the only permissible paid action would be boosting a single proven organic winner to warm retargeting audiences (past page engagers and event RSVPs), not cold audiences. Run Template 4 (micro-lift check, "how did you hear about us?") in the conversion flow before committing any budget.

**Frequency cap:** not applicable in the absence of paid campaigns. For organic Facebook posts shared into groups, the campaign manager must track how frequently the same content appears in the same groups — once a post has been reshared 3+ times in the same group in 7 days, it reads as spam and reduces reach. Rotate content.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template 5 — Organic-source attribution (zero-budget campaign)**

This is the correct template at categorical asymmetry. There is no paid holdout to test — the measurement question is: which organic channels are converting, and how should the campaign reallocate effort based on the answer?

**Hypothesis:** Among the candidate's organic channels (Facebook personal page, Facebook Groups, Discord, TikTok, earned media), at least one channel will outperform the others by ≥5× on conversion rate per unique visitor, indicating where the campaign should concentrate effort in Months 2–6.

**Setup:**
- Every link the campaign publishes carries UTM parameters: `utm_source=[linkedin|facebook-personal|facebook-group-[name]|discord|tiktok|press-[outlet-slug]|volunteer-referral]`, `utm_medium=organic`, `utm_campaign=mayoral-6mo`.
- "How did you hear about us?" field in the volunteer sign-up form and Discord join flow with a short-list of options matching the UTM sources.
- Analytics: Plausible or PostHog with goal events: volunteer sign-up, Discord join, platform document download, event RSVP.

**Duration:** 30-day initial window (Weeks 1–4). Review at end of Week 4.

**Metrics:** Per-channel conversion rate = (conversions from channel ÷ unique visitors from channel). Rank all channels.

**Decision rules:**
- Top channel: double weekly effort in Month 2.
- Any channel below 20% of top channel's conversion rate: drop unless it serves a secondary function (credibility signal, endorser relationship, press contact) the conversion metric cannot see. Name the secondary function explicitly or drop the channel.
- Middle-band channels: maintain effort, retest after any format change.
- If all channels show conversion rate <0.5%: the bottleneck is upstream — review the positioning line, the call-to-action clarity, and the MMF Question 2 language gap identified in Stage 3a.

**Incremental metric:** volunteer sign-ups per week by channel (not impressions, not followers, not likes). This is the only metric that indicates the campaign is actually building the ground network it needs.

**This is directional, not causal.** The template is honest about its limits: multi-touch attribution is underspecified, and someone who joins Discord after seeing a TikTok will be counted as a Discord conversion. Use the rankings as a rough guide to effort allocation, not as proof of causal lift. If any paid campaign becomes permissible in a later phase, graduate to Template 1 (geo-holdout) for real incremental measurement.

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

### Track these

| Metric | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Volunteer contact rate per precinct (doors knocked ÷ registered voters in precinct) | The ground game's coverage metric — the only number that predicts turnout in targeted precincts |
| Per-channel conversion rate to volunteer sign-up (Template 5 output) | Tells the campaign where to allocate time, not where to feel good |
| Discord member activity rate (members who posted or replied in the last 7 days ÷ total members) | Distinguishes a live community from a broadcast channel in disguise |
| Volunteer retention (active last week ÷ total ever signed up) | If volunteers stop showing up, the ground game collapses — surface early |
| New voter contacts logged per week (CRM) | The direct pipeline metric to election-day turnout |
| Organic content saves + shares (not likes) | Saves and shares indicate the audience found the content worth keeping or spreading — likes are cheap and lie |
| Independent press pickups (earned media placements in non-incumbent-aligned outlets) | Credibility borrowed from the independent press reaches segments the campaign's owned channels cannot |
| Voter segment conversion (how many contacts in each key segment — young professionals, small business owners, public-sector workers, retirees — have moved from "aware" to "committed supporter" in the CRM) | The only segmented metric that tells whether the campaign is persuading its target audiences or just activating already-committed voters |

### Ignore these

| Metric | Why it is a trap |
|---|---|
| Total Facebook Page followers | Organic reach on Facebook Pages is ~0.15% — a follower count is not an audience. Track the 5 Facebook Group engagement rates instead. |
| Total impressions and CPM | The campaign is not buying media. Impressions are a vanity metric when the primary channel is ground contact. |
| Raw Facebook or TikTok likes | Likes do not indicate persuasion and are easy to inflate (bots, casual scrollers). Saves and shares are the signal. |
| National poll movement | National polls for a local race are too lagged, too noisy, and too aggregated to be actionable. Track precinct-level volunteer contact rate instead. |
| Social media follower count | The 12k Facebook followers are a starting asset, not a goal. Follower count growth during the campaign does not predict votes. |
| Platform-reported ad reach or attributed conversions | Not applicable — no paid campaign. If any paid test is run, refuse to report platform-attributed ROAS; use Template 4 micro-lift check instead. |
| "Engagement rate" without segmentation | Average engagement rate across all content mixes high-signal content (policy specifics, earned-media moments) with low-signal content (rally footage that activates already-committed supporters). Segment by content type and audience before drawing conclusions. |

**Sector failure mode (from Rider 5):** The common failure in political/civic campaigns is fragmented opposition — multiple small campaigns diluting the organic narrative and benefiting the consolidated incumbent. This campaign is currently the single consolidated challenger, which is a precondition for the playbook working. If any other candidate enters the race, the campaign's first response should be to assess coalition consolidation before adjusting the channel strategy.

---

*Confirm or adjust any row in the Assumptions table (Section 0) and the affected stages will be updated.*
